Paper.title: Phase Field Simulation for Dendrite Growth in Energy Storage Batteries

Model.name: General phase-field framework for dendrite growth
Model.description: Defines phase-field variables to represent phase states and couples conserved concentration fields through free-energy variational derivatives; used to simulate transformation from electrolyte phase to electrode phase and evolving electrode–electrolyte interface during dendrite growth.
Model.evidence: Basic framework introduces phase-field variables ξα evolved by Allen–Cahn equation and concentration variables ci evolved by Cahn–Hilliard equation; total free energy F includes chemical, electrostatic, elastic (and other) terms. :contentReference[oaicite:0]{index=0}

Model.name: Electrochemical phase-field model coupling phase field, ion concentration, and electric field
Model.model_type: Phase-field electrochemical model (control equations; Poisson/Nernst–Planck; Butler–Volmer variants)
Model.description: Couples phase-field evolution with ion transport and electric potential to simulate metal deposition and dendrite growth; emphasizes control equations linking free-energy driving force and evolution of phase field, ion concentration, and electric potential.
Model.evidence: Paper reviews evolution of control equations (Guyer 2003; Shibuta 2006; nonlinear 2012; Chen 2015) to couple ξ, ion concentrations, and electric potential for electrodeposition/dendrite growth. :contentReference[oaicite:1]{index=1}

Model.name: Temperature-coupled phase-field model for dendrite growth
Model.description: Adds temperature field via heat transfer equation; later work couples temperature effects through temperature-dependent transport/parameters and internal heat generation.
Model.evidence: Temperature field coupling described (Assadi 2007; Yan 2018 coupling Chen model with heat transfer equation). :contentReference[oaicite:2]{index=2}

Model.name: Stress/elasticity-coupled phase-field model for dendrite growth
Model.model_type: Phase-field + elastic strain energy + mechanical equilibrium
Model.description: Incorporates elastic strain energy into total free energy and couples mechanical equilibrium to compute local stress tensor and stress evolution during dendrite growth, including effects in solid electrolytes and crack–dendrite interactions.
Model.evidence: Elastic strain energy term and mechanical equilibrium equations presented; stress field used to study suppression by high modulus/external stress and effects of cracks in solid electrolytes. :contentReference[oaicite:3]{index=3}

Model.name: SEI-aware phase-field modeling (noise term / geometric reconstruction / current correction)
Model.model_type: Phase-field with SEI represented implicitly or structurally
Model.description: Represents SEI influence on dendrite growth via (i) geometric reconstruction of porous SEI, (ii) current correction factors capturing SEI heterogeneity, or (iii) an added noise term in the free-energy functional to implicitly represent SEI granularity/heterogeneity.
Model.evidence: SEI effects incorporated via geometric reconstruction, current correction factor, or noise term f_ns in free energy functional (Eq. 18). :contentReference[oaicite:4]{index=4}

Model.name: Anisotropy-inclusive phase-field model for dendrite growth
Model.description: Includes anisotropy through orientation-dependent interfacial thickness or surface energy function k(θ), enabling realistic branching/coarsening and morphology changes (e.g., tip-splitting vs needle-like tips).
Model.evidence: Anisotropy incorporated into Allen–Cahn formulation and via surface energy anisotropy function k(θ)=k0[1+δcos(λθ)] (Eq. 20). :contentReference[oaicite:5]{index=5}

Equation.name: Allen–Cahn equation for phase variables
Equation.description: Evolution equation for non-conserved phase-field variables describing phase states; Lαβ is interface mobility; f is free-energy density.
Equation.evidence: Allen–Cahn evolution for phases with interface mobility Lαβ. :contentReference[oaicite:6]{index=6}

Equation.name: Cahn–Hilliard equation for conserved concentrations
Equation.description: Evolution equation for conserved concentration variables in absence of chemical reactions; Mij is atomic mobility between components i and j.
Equation.evidence: Cahn–Hilliard equation for conserved concentration variables. :contentReference[oaicite:7]{index=7}

Equation.name: Total free energy functional
Equation.expression: F = ∫V f dV = ∫V (f_chem + f_elec + f_elas + …) dV
Equation.description: Total free energy expressed as integral of multiple energy contributions (chemical, electrostatic, elastic, etc.) as functions of defined field variables.
Equation.evidence: Total free energy includes fchem, felec, felas and other terms (Eq. 3). :contentReference[oaicite:8]{index=8}

Equation.name: Guyer-type phase-field control equation (electrochemical, early simplified model)
Equation.description: Early electrochemical phase-field equation derived with ideal-solution assumptions and simplified kinetics/charge distribution; includes interpolation p(ξ), double-well g(ξ), gradient energy kξ, dielectric ε(ξ), and electric potential φ contributions.
Equation.evidence: Control equation for ξ in Guyer et al. model (Eq. 4) with described simplifications. :contentReference[oaicite:9]{index=9}

Equation.name: Guyer-type ion flux and conservation
Equation.description: Simplified ion diffusion using linear kinetic assumption; ignores complex interactions between ions in early model.
Equation.evidence: Ion diffusion/flux equations in Guyer model (Eq. 5). :contentReference[oaicite:10]{index=10}

Equation.name: Poisson equation for potential balance
Equation.description: Maintains potential balance given dielectric ε(ξ) and charge density ρ; early model treats charge distribution as uniform.
Equation.evidence: Poisson equation used for potential balance (Eq. 6) with idealized charge distribution. :contentReference[oaicite:11]{index=11}

Equation.name: Nonlinear phase-field governing equation with electrochemical reactions
Equation.description: Nonlinear phase-field equation including two driving factors: interfacial free energy and electrochemical reaction kinetics; includes reaction rate constant Lη and weighting coefficient h(ξ).
Equation.evidence: Nonlinear model equation for ξ includes free energy and electrochemical reaction driving factors (Eq. 9). :contentReference[oaicite:12]{index=12}

Equation.name: Nonlinear Li+ transport with reaction source term
Equation.description: Ion diffusion equation considering accumulation/depletion of Li+ due to interfacial reactions during dendrite growth.
Equation.evidence: Ion diffusion equation includes reaction source term Ṙ_Li+ (Eq. 10). :contentReference[oaicite:13]{index=13}

Equation.name: Chen-type phase-field equation with modified Butler–Volmer driving
Equation.expression: ∂ξ/∂t = -Lσ( p'(ξ) - k∇²ξ ) - Lη h'(ξ) { exp(-nFηa/RT) - c̃+ exp(αnFηa/RT) }
Equation.description: Phase-field evolution includes free-energy gradient term for interfacial structure and electrochemical reaction term driven by overpotential ηa; integrates Butler–Volmer-like kinetics.
Equation.evidence: Model integrates Butler–Volmer, Nernst–Planck, and charge neutrality to solve ξ, potential, and concentration fields (Eq. 11). :contentReference[oaicite:14]{index=14}

Equation.name: Nernst–Planck transport with charge neutrality coupling
Equation.description: Couples lithium-ion concentration fields with electric field (via potential Φ) using Nernst–Planck form and charge neutrality condition; accounts for reduction/consumption associated with Li in anode.
Equation.evidence: Equation 12 described as Nernst–Planck with charge neutrality coupling between ion concentration and electric field. :contentReference[oaicite:15]{index=15}

Equation.name: Temperature evolution equation (Assadi-type coupling)
Equation.description: Couples temperature field with phase/solute evolution via thermal diffusion and enthalpy term; early version does not include internal heat generation process.
Equation.evidence: Assadi temperature field evolution equation provided (Eq. 13) with noted limitations. :contentReference[oaicite:16]{index=16}

Equation.name: Heat transfer equation with internal heat generation
Equation.description: Heat transfer model including effective thermal conductivity and internal heat production rate; coupled to phase-field through diffusion coefficient in reviewed approach.
Equation.evidence: Yan et al. coupled Chen phase-field model with heat transfer equation including internal heat production Q (Eq. 14). :contentReference[oaicite:17]{index=17}

Equation.name: Elastic strain energy density term
Equation.description: Elastic strain energy density incorporated into total free energy functional to model mechanical effects during dendrite growth.

Equation.name: Mechanical equilibrium (stress divergence free)
Equation.description: Mechanical equilibrium equation solved to obtain stress distribution during dendrite growth (electrolyte/anode treated as linear elastic in reviewed model).

Equation.name: Linear elastic constitutive relation with eigenstrain
Equation.description: Local stress tensor computed from elastic constants, total strain, and eigenstrain.

Equation.name: Free energy functional including SEI noise term
Equation.description: Adds noise term f_ns to total free energy to implicitly represent SEI influence on dendrite growth.
Equation.evidence: Noise term added to free-energy functional to represent SEI effects (Eq. 18). :contentReference[oaicite:21]{index=21}

Equation.name: Surface energy anisotropy function
Equation.description: Orientation-dependent surface energy function used to incorporate anisotropy; θ is interface normal angle; δ anisotropy strength; λ anisotropy mode.

Term.name: Gradient energy (interfacial) term
Term.description: Interfacial energy contribution improving capture of fine interfacial structures in phase-field evolution equations.
Term.evidence: Gradient term included in phase-field free-energy component and Chen-type equation description. :contentReference[oaicite:23]{index=23}

Term.name: Electrochemical reaction term driven by overpotential
Term.description: Reaction kinetics contribution in modified Butler–Volmer form affecting deposition rate and dendrite growth under high current density conditions.
Term.evidence: Electrochemical reaction term described as depending on overpotential ηa in Chen-type model (Eq. 11). :contentReference[oaicite:24]{index=24}

Term.name: Internal heat generation term
Term.description: Internal heat production rate in heat transfer equation used for temperature-coupled phase-field simulations.
Term.evidence: Heat transfer equation includes internal heat production rate Q (Eq. 14). :contentReference[oaicite:25]{index=25}

Term.name: Noise term representing SEI influence
Term.description: Noise term added to free energy to implicitly account for SEI heterogeneity/granularity effects on dendritic evolution.
Term.evidence: Noise term f_ns in SEI-inclusive free energy functional (Eq. 18). :contentReference[oaicite:26]{index=26}

Variable.name: Phase-field variable
Variable.physical_meaning: Order parameter describing phase states (electrode vs electrolyte) and diffuse interface evolution.
Variable.evidence: Phase-field variables ξα define phase states; interface evolution described via Allen–Cahn equation. :contentReference[oaicite:27]{index=27}

Variable.name: Component concentration
Variable.physical_meaning: Concentration of chemical species/components coupled to chemical free energy and interfacial processes.
Variable.evidence: Concentration variables ci introduced; evolve via Cahn–Hilliard equation when no chemical reaction. :contentReference[oaicite:28]{index=28}

Variable.name: Electric potential
Variable.physical_meaning: Electric potential satisfying Poisson equation and coupling to ion migration and electrochemical reactions.
Variable.evidence: Electric potential φ appears in Poisson equation and electrochemical phase-field control equations. :contentReference[oaicite:29]{index=29}

Variable.name: Charge density
Variable.physical_meaning: Charge density term in Poisson equation maintaining potential balance.

Variable.name: Lithium-ion concentration
Variable.physical_meaning: Lithium ion concentration field; can be depleted near anode at high voltage and drives dendrite growth through deposition kinetics.
Variable.evidence: Ion concentration field is key factor; Li+ distribution couples with phase-field variable and electric field; depletion near anode under high voltage discussed. :contentReference[oaicite:31]{index=31}

Variable.name: Overpotential
Variable.physical_meaning: Electrochemical overpotential driving deposition reactions and dendrite growth rate/branching.
Variable.evidence: Overpotential appears in modified Butler–Volmer term; dendrite tip exhibits relatively high overpotential and increasing overpotential accelerates growth/branching. :contentReference[oaicite:32]{index=32}

Variable.name: Temperature
Variable.physical_meaning: Temperature field affecting diffusion, conductivity, and dendrite morphology/size; included via heat transfer equation.
Variable.evidence: Temperature field coupled via temperature evolution/heat transfer equations; increasing temperature often reduces dendrite size/branching in reviewed studies. :contentReference[oaicite:33]{index=33}

Variable.name: Stress tensor
Variable.physical_meaning: Local stress tensor obtained from mechanical equilibrium and constitutive relation; affects dendrite morphology and crack interactions.
Variable.evidence: Stress tensor computed via σij,j=0 and σij=Cijkl(εij-ε0ij); stress distribution and suppressive effect of high modulus/external stress discussed. :contentReference[oaicite:34]{index=34}

Parameter.name: Interface mobility
Parameter.physical_meaning: Mobility between phase α and phase β controlling phase-field evolution rate.
Parameter.evidence: Lαβ represents interface mobility in Allen–Cahn equation. :contentReference[oaicite:35]{index=35}

Parameter.name: Atomic mobility
Parameter.physical_meaning: Mobility between components i and j in Cahn–Hilliard transport.
Parameter.evidence: Mij is atomic mobility between components i and j in Cahn–Hilliard equation. :contentReference[oaicite:36]{index=36}

Parameter.name: Gradient energy coefficient
Parameter.physical_meaning: Coefficient for gradient energy term controlling interface energy/thickness in phase-field evolution.
Parameter.evidence: kξ identified as gradient energy coefficient of phase field in Guyer model description. :contentReference[oaicite:37]{index=37}

Parameter.name: Dielectric constant
Parameter.physical_meaning: Phase-dependent dielectric constant used in electrostatic energy and Poisson equation.
Parameter.evidence: ε(ξ) is dielectric constant in Poisson equation and Guyer control equation. :contentReference[oaicite:38]{index=38}

Parameter.name: Faraday constant
Parameter.physical_meaning: Electrochemical constant coupling charge and moles; appears in electrochemical kinetics/transport scaling.
Parameter.evidence: Faraday constant F appears in reviewed nonlinear and Chen-type models. :contentReference[oaicite:39]{index=39}

Parameter.name: Gas constant
Parameter.physical_meaning: Thermodynamic constant used in electrochemical kinetics expressions.
Parameter.evidence: Gas constant R appears in Shibuta/Chen/temperature-related formulations. :contentReference[oaicite:40]{index=40}

Parameter.name: Thermal diffusivity
Parameter.physical_meaning: Thermal diffusivity in temperature evolution equation.
Parameter.evidence: αth is thermal diffusivity in Assadi temperature equation. :contentReference[oaicite:41]{index=41}

Parameter.name: Effective thermal conductivity
Parameter.physical_meaning: Effective thermal conductivity in heat transfer equation.
Parameter.evidence: Ra is effective thermal conductivity in heat transfer equation (Eq. 14). :contentReference[oaicite:42]{index=42}

Parameter.name: Internal heat production rate
Parameter.physical_meaning: Internal heat generation term in heat transfer equation.
Parameter.evidence: Q appears as internal heat production rate in Eq. 14. :contentReference[oaicite:43]{index=43}

Parameter.name: Elastic constant tensor
Parameter.physical_meaning: Elastic stiffness constants used to compute elastic energy and stress from strain.
Parameter.evidence: Cijkl is elastic constant in elastic strain energy and constitutive relation (Eqs. 15–17). :contentReference[oaicite:44]{index=44}

Parameter.name: Surface energy (baseline)
Parameter.physical_meaning: Baseline surface energy in anisotropy function k(θ).
Parameter.evidence: k0 is surface energy in anisotropy function (Eq. 20). :contentReference[oaicite:45]{index=45}

Parameter.name: Anisotropy strength
Parameter.physical_meaning: Strength of anisotropy in surface energy function.
Parameter.evidence: δ is anisotropic strength in k(θ)=k0[1+δcos(λθ)] (Eq. 20). :contentReference[oaicite:46]{index=46}

Parameter.name: Anisotropy mode
Parameter.physical_meaning: Mode of anisotropy in surface energy function.
Parameter.evidence: λ is mode of anisotropy in Eq. 20. :contentReference[oaicite:47]{index=47}

PhysicalField.name: Phase field
PhysicalField.description: Diffuse interface/order parameter field distinguishing electrode and electrolyte phases and tracking interface evolution.
PhysicalField.evidence: Phase-field variable used to represent phase states and interface transition region in dendrite growth. :contentReference[oaicite:48]{index=48}

PhysicalField.name: Ion concentration field
PhysicalField.description: Spatial distribution of ions (e.g., Li+) coupled to electrodeposition and dendrite morphology; can deplete near anode at high voltage.
PhysicalField.evidence: Ion distribution discussed as key factor; concentration profiles and spatial resemblance to dendritic morphology referenced. :contentReference[oaicite:49]{index=49}

PhysicalField.name: Electric potential/electric field
PhysicalField.description: Electric potential field governing migration and electrochemical kinetics; overpotential drives dendrite growth rate and branching.
PhysicalField.evidence: Applied electric field/overpotential described as driving force and coupled in control equations (Poisson/Nernst–Planck/Butler–Volmer). :contentReference[oaicite:50]{index=50}

PhysicalField.name: Temperature field
PhysicalField.description: Temperature distribution affecting diffusion, conductivity, and dendrite growth morphology and size.
PhysicalField.evidence: Temperature field introduced via heat transfer equation and temperature-dependent parameters. :contentReference[oaicite:51]{index=51}

PhysicalField.name: Stress/mechanical field
PhysicalField.description: Mechanical stress field in electrode/electrolyte influencing dendrite morphology, branching, and crack interactions in solid electrolytes.
PhysicalField.evidence: Stress field modeled via elastic energy and mechanical equilibrium; high modulus/external stress suppress dendrites; crack interactions discussed. :contentReference[oaicite:52]{index=52}

EnergyTerm.name: Chemical free energy
EnergyTerm.description: Chemical energy contribution to total free energy functional driving diffusion and phase evolution.

EnergyTerm.name: Electrostatic energy
EnergyTerm.description: Electrostatic energy contribution to total free energy; couples to electric potential and dielectric properties.
EnergyTerm.evidence: Total free energy includes felec (Eq. 3) and Poisson equation coupling. :contentReference[oaicite:54]{index=54}

EnergyTerm.name: Elastic strain energy
EnergyTerm.description: Elastic energy contribution to total free energy capturing mechanical effects during dendrite growth.
EnergyTerm.evidence: Total free energy includes felas (Eq. 3) and elastic strain energy expression (Eq. 15). :contentReference[oaicite:55]{index=55}

EnergyTerm.name: Noise energy term (SEI representation)
EnergyTerm.description: Noise term added to free energy to implicitly represent SEI influence on dendritic evolution.
EnergyTerm.evidence: Noise term fns added in SEI-inclusive free energy functional (Eq. 18). :contentReference[oaicite:56]{index=56}

Phenomenon.name: Lithium dendrite growth
Phenomenon.description: Uneven metal ion deposition leading to dendrites on electrode surface during battery cycling, potentially causing local high current density and short circuits.
Phenomenon.evidence: Dendrite formation and hazards described in introduction; phase-field models reviewed for dendrite growth in energy storage batteries. :contentReference[oaicite:57]{index=57}

Phenomenon.name: Electrochemical metal electrodeposition
Phenomenon.description: Electrochemical deposition processes in which ion transport and reactions drive metal growth, including dendritic morphologies.
Phenomenon.evidence: Phase-field models applied to electrodeposition/dendrite growth; control equations couple ion concentration and electric field. :contentReference[oaicite:58]{index=58}

Phenomenon.name: Dead lithium formation
Phenomenon.description: Formation of electrically isolated lithium during stripping/cycling, reducing active lithium area and causing overpotential/polarization and capacity loss.
Phenomenon.evidence: Dead lithium analysis section describes phase-field simulations for plating/stripping and factors affecting dead lithium. :contentReference[oaicite:59]{index=59}

Phenomenon.name: Dendrite–crack interaction in solid electrolytes
Phenomenon.description: Interaction between lithium dendrites and microcracks in solid-state electrolytes, including stress concentration effects, crack propagation, and dendrite infiltration.
Phenomenon.evidence: Solid-state electrolyte microcracks and dendrite interaction discussed, including stress concentrations and crack-tip effects. :contentReference[oaicite:60]{index=60}

Morphology.name: Mossy
Morphology.description: Mossy dendrite morphology (often early-stage; can be needle-like/fibrous initially).
Morphology.evidence: Morphology evolution described and morphology categories listed; mossy included. :contentReference[oaicite:61]{index=61}

Morphology.name: Tree-like (dendritic)
Morphology.description: Tree-like dendritic morphology with branching; can increase with applied voltage/overpotential and other conditions.
Morphology.evidence: Electric field/overpotential increases branching leading to tree-like structures; morphology categories include dendritic/tree-type. :contentReference[oaicite:62]{index=62}

Morphology.name: Needle-like
Morphology.description: Needle-like dendrite morphology; can arise with higher anisotropy or other conditions.
Morphology.evidence: Needle-like morphology referenced as influenced by anisotropy and listed among observed morphologies. :contentReference[oaicite:63]{index=63}

Morphology.name: Spherical
Morphology.description: Spherical deposition/dendrite nuclei morphology; can be observed under certain temperature gradients/conditions.
Morphology.evidence: Morphology shift to spherical with higher temperature gradient; spherical listed among morphologies. :contentReference[oaicite:64]{index=64}

Morphology.name: Fibrous
Morphology.description: Fibrous morphology; reported to occur with increasing temperature in reviewed statements.
Morphology.evidence: With increasing temperature, morphology evolves from tip-splitting tree-like to fibrous. :contentReference[oaicite:65]{index=65}

Morphology.name: Bush-like (shrub-like)
Morphology.description: Bush-like/shrub-like morphology; associated with granular/heterogeneous SEI representations in models.
Morphology.evidence: Granular SEI can induce transition from filamentous to bush-like; noise field modeling yields shrub-like filaments consistent with experiments. :contentReference[oaicite:66]{index=66}

Morphology.name: Tip-splitting tree-like
Morphology.description: Tip-splitting dendritic pattern; reported at certain temperatures or low anisotropy conditions.
Morphology.evidence: Increasing temperature changes morphology from tip-splitting tree-like to fibrous; low anisotropy leads dendrite tip splitting. :contentReference[oaicite:67]{index=67}

InfluencingFactor.name: Ion distribution / electrolyte concentration
InfluencingFactor.description: Ion concentration field and its spatial distribution; depletion near anode at high voltage; higher electrolyte concentration can reduce dendrite size; designed concentration gradients can hinder propagation.
InfluencingFactor.evidence: Ion concentration field described as key factor; electrolyte concentration and spatial distribution effects summarized. :contentReference[oaicite:68]{index=68}

InfluencingFactor.name: Applied electric field / overpotential
InfluencingFactor.description: Applied voltage/overpotential drives deposition; higher overpotential increases growth rate and branching; pulsed voltage can reduce dendrite dimensions versus constant voltage.
InfluencingFactor.evidence: Electric field drives morphology change and concentration depletion; overpotential increases growth/branching; pulsed voltage discussed. :contentReference[oaicite:69]{index=69}

InfluencingFactor.name: Temperature / temperature gradient
InfluencingFactor.description: Temperature affects diffusion and morphology; many studies show increasing temperature reduces dendrite size and branching; higher temperature gradient can shift morphology toward spherical forms.
InfluencingFactor.evidence: Temperature field influence and coupling reviewed; qualitative morphology changes and suppression trends described. :contentReference[oaicite:70]{index=70}

InfluencingFactor.name: Mechanical stress / external pressure / electrolyte modulus
InfluencingFactor.description: Stress field affects dendrite trunk/branching and can cause fracture; higher electrolyte mechanical strength and greater external stress suppress dendrite growth; cracks in solid electrolytes modify morphology and facilitate infiltration.
InfluencingFactor.evidence: Stress effects summarized (thinner trunk/less branches/fracture); models coupling elasticity discussed; suppression by high modulus/stress and crack interactions described. :contentReference[oaicite:71]{index=71}

InfluencingFactor.name: Solid electrolyte interphase (SEI)
InfluencingFactor.description: SEI can act as barrier or introduce heterogeneity; granular SEI can shift filamentous to bush-like; modeled via geometric reconstruction, current correction factors, or noise term in free energy.
InfluencingFactor.evidence: SEI role and modeling approaches reviewed; SEI can suppress dendrites; granular nature affects morphology. :contentReference[oaicite:72]{index=72}

InfluencingFactor.name: Anisotropy (surface energy / diffusion / structure)
InfluencingFactor.description: Anisotropy affects nucleation sites, number of trunks, and growth rates; higher anisotropy can shift morphology from mossy to tree-like/needle-like and produce needle-like tips.
InfluencingFactor.evidence: Anisotropy described as key factor; k(θ) formulation and morphology effects summarized. :contentReference[oaicite:73]{index=73}

InfluencingFactor.name: Separator pore size and thickness (geometry)
InfluencingFactor.description: Smaller separator pores promote more uniform electrode-surface ion concentrations and smoother deposition; thicker separators yield more uniform growth and lower growth rates.
InfluencingFactor.evidence: Electrode structure optimization section summarizes pore size and separator thickness effects on dendrites. :contentReference[oaicite:74]{index=74}

MaterialSystem.description: Review discusses lithium metal batteries and solid-state lithium metal batteries in general, focusing on dendrite growth in energy storage batteries.
MaterialSystem.evidence: Paper discusses lithium metal as ideal anode and phase-field simulations for lithium metal batteries and solid-state lithium metal batteries. :contentReference[oaicite:75]{index=75}

SimulationResult.description: Qualitative morphology transitions: increasing applied voltage changes from mossy/needle-like/fibrous to tree-like with increased branching; granular SEI can shift filamentous to bush-like; increasing temperature shifts tip-splitting tree-like to fibrous and higher temperature gradient shifts tree-like to spherical; stress reduces side branches and can fracture trunk; increased anisotropy increases nucleation sites/trunks and shifts morphology toward tree-like/needle-like.
SimulationResult.evidence: Paper summarizes morphology effects of electric field, SEI, temperature, stress, anisotropy on lithium dendrites. :contentReference[oaicite:76]{index=76}

SimulationResult.description: Higher overpotential increases dendrite growth rate and intensifies branching/instability; dendrite tips exhibit relatively high overpotential; pulsed voltage produces shorter, narrower dendrites compared to constant voltage under equivalent deposition capacity/average current rate.
SimulationResult.evidence: Overpotential discussed as key variable; increased overpotential accelerates growth and branching; pulsed voltage reduces dendrite dimensions. :contentReference[oaicite:77]{index=77}

SimulationResult.description: Many reviewed studies report increasing temperature reduces dendrite size and decreases branching; temperature regulation suggested as dendrite suppression strategy (e.g., adjusting temperature near anode for uniform deposition).
SimulationResult.evidence: Temperature-coupled model section summarizes studies showing reduced size/branching at higher temperatures and proposes temperature regulation for suppression. :contentReference[oaicite:78]{index=78}

SimulationResult.description: Higher mechanical strength (Young’s modulus) of electrolyte and greater external stress suppress dendrite growth; in solid electrolytes, microcracks interact with dendrites: cracks can expand under dendritic stress, stress concentrations at crack tips/voids alter morphology, and severe crack propagation correlates with larger dendritic growth areas.
SimulationResult.evidence: Stress field section summarizes suppression and crack–dendrite interaction conclusions and mitigation strategies. :contentReference[oaicite:79]{index=79}

SimulationResult.description: SEI layer can suppress dendrite development; porous/denser SEI structures inhibit dendrite growth in reconstructed models; SEI heterogeneity can be represented by current correction or noise term in free energy; chemical characteristics of SEI increasingly incorporated to improve realism.
SimulationResult.evidence: SEI section describes suppression effects and modeling approaches including Eq. 18 noise term and geometric reconstruction. :contentReference[oaicite:80]{index=80}

InfluenceStatement.effect: Ion concentration depletion near anode under high applied voltage is associated with increased branching; increasing electrolyte concentration can reduce dendrite size; engineered concentration gradients can hinder propagation.
InfluenceStatement.mechanism: Ion transport limits and spatial concentration gradients near electrode affect deposition rate and morphology; depletion at tip/interface promotes instability/branching.
InfluenceStatement.description: Ion concentration is a crucial factor; regulation of electrolyte ion distribution is suggested for suppressing dendrites.
InfluenceStatement.evidence: Paper states ion depletion near anode under high voltage; higher electrolyte concentration reduces dendrite size; designed concentration gradients hinder propagation. :contentReference[oaicite:81]{index=81}

InfluenceStatement.effect: Increasing applied voltage/overpotential accelerates dendrite growth rate and increases branching/instability; pulsed voltage reduces dendrite height/width versus constant voltage.
InfluenceStatement.mechanism: Overpotential directly drives electrochemical reaction rate in control equations, making high-current-density deposition more unstable and branched.
InfluenceStatement.description: Overpotential is primary driving force for dendrite growth; protocol optimization (pulse charging frequency/voltage) can regulate dendritic behavior.
InfluenceStatement.evidence: Paper summarizes overpotential at dendrite tip and increased growth/branching with higher overpotential, and pulsed-voltage suppression effects. :contentReference[oaicite:82]{index=82}

InfluenceStatement.effect: Increasing temperature tends to reduce dendrite size and branching; morphology evolves from tip-splitting tree-like to fibrous with increasing temperature; higher temperature gradient shifts morphology toward spherical.
InfluenceStatement.mechanism: Temperature field coupled via heat transfer equation and temperature-dependent transport/parameters (e.g., diffusion coefficients, conductivity, surface tension), influencing ion transport and interface dynamics.
InfluenceStatement.description: Temperature regulation is proposed as a dendrite suppression strategy in reviewed works.
InfluenceStatement.evidence: Paper describes temperature-driven morphology transitions and reports many studies showing reduced size/branching at higher temperature and gradient-driven spherical tendency. :contentReference[oaicite:83]{index=83}

InfluenceStatement.factor: Mechanical stress / external pressure / electrolyte modulus
InfluenceStatement.effect: Applied stress produces thinner main trunk and reduces side branches, can fracture trunk; higher electrolyte strength (Young’s modulus) and larger external stress suppress growth; cracks strongly influence dendrite behavior and can promote infiltration.
InfluenceStatement.mechanism: Elastic energy and mechanical equilibrium determine stress distribution; stress concentrations at roots/crack tips alter growth direction and can cause fracture/penetration.
InfluenceStatement.description: Mechanical strengthening and external compression are suggested to limit dendrite penetration, especially in solid electrolytes with defects.
InfluenceStatement.evidence: Paper summarizes stress effects on morphology and general suppression by high modulus/external stress; crack–dendrite interactions described. :contentReference[oaicite:84]{index=84}

InfluenceStatement.effect: SEI may suppress dendritic development as barrier; granular/heterogeneous SEI can change morphology (e.g., filamentous to bush-like); SEI modeling via reconstruction/correction/noise captures coarse effects, while incorporating SEI chemistry is a developing direction.
InfluenceStatement.mechanism: SEI structure/porosity/heterogeneity modifies ion distribution and local kinetics; noise term f_ns used to represent stochastic heterogeneity; chemical composition can homogenize fields and strengthen SEI.
InfluenceStatement.description: Incorporating chemical characteristics of SEI into phase-field models is proposed for improved suppression design.
InfluenceStatement.evidence: SEI section discusses barrier role, porosity reconstruction, current correction, and noise term in free energy (Eq. 18), plus chemical SEI examples. :contentReference[oaicite:85]{index=85}

InfluenceStatement.effect: Increasing anisotropy increases nucleation sites and number of trunks; morphology changes from mossy to tree-like or needle-like; low anisotropy promotes tip splitting, higher anisotropy yields needle-like tips and faster growth.
InfluenceStatement.mechanism: Orientation-dependent surface energy/interfacial thickness affects interface stability and branching/coarsening dynamics.
InfluenceStatement.description: Anisotropy is treated as key factor and incorporated via k(θ) formulation.
InfluenceStatement.evidence: Paper states morphology transitions with anisotropy and presents anisotropy function k(θ)=k0[1+δcos(λθ)] (Eq. 20). :contentReference[oaicite:86]{index=86}

ontology_reference: Lithium Dendrite Modeling Ontology (provided by user). :contentReference[oaicite:87]{index=87}

domain_filter.reason: Paper explicitly focuses on phase-field simulation of dendrite growth in energy storage batteries (including lithium dendrites), discussing influencing factors, governing equations, and applications.
